chapter: Chapter 3: Electricity: Circuits and their Components

questions.question: If two bulbs are connected in series and one bulb is removed, what will happen to the circuit?

options.C: The circuit becomes open and no bulb glows

questions.explanation: In a series circuit, removing one component breaks the path, stopping current flow.

questions.question: Which of the following materials would provide the least resistance to electric current?
questions.explanation: Copper is a good conductor and offers very low resistance to current.

questions.question: Why is a fuse made of a thin wire with a low melting point?

options.B: To melt quickly during excess current
options.C: To increase resistance in normal circuits

questions.explanation: A fuse wire melts when current exceeds a safe limit, protecting the circuit.

questions.question: What will happen to the brightness of bulbs if identical bulbs are connected in parallel?
questions.explanation: In parallel circuits, each bulb receives the full voltage, so brightness remains the same.

questions.question: Which factor does NOT affect the resistance of a wire?
questions.explanation: Resistance depends on length, thickness, and material, not color.

questions.question: If resistance in a circuit doubles while voltage remains constant, current will:
questions.explanation: From Ohm’s law (I = V/R), doubling resistance halves the current.

questions.question: Why are copper wires commonly used in household wiring?
questions.explanation: Copper has excellent conductivity and is flexible, making it ideal for wiring.

questions.question: Which circuit arrangement is used in homes so that appliances work independently?
questions.explanation: Parallel circuits allow appliances to operate independently.

questions.question: What causes a short circuit in electrical wiring?

options.C: Direct contact between live and neutral wires

questions.explanation: Direct contact between live and neutral wires causes a short circuit and excessive current.

questions.question: Which device automatically switches off during electrical overload?
questions.explanation: An MCB automatically trips and breaks the circuit during overload.

questions.question: Which part of an electric bulb produces light?
questions.explanation: The filament heats up and emits light when current flows through it.

questions.question: What is the role of earthing in electrical systems?
questions.explanation: Earthing directs excess current safely into the ground to prevent shocks.

questions.question: If a bulb glows dimly, which change would increase its brightness?
questions.explanation: Higher voltage increases current through the filament, increasing brightness.

questions.question: Which of the following is an insulator used in electrical wiring?
questions.explanation: PVC is used as insulation to prevent current leakage.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in ohms (Ω).

questions.question: If the thickness of a wire increases, resistance will:
questions.explanation: Thicker wires allow easier flow of electrons, reducing resistance.

questions.question: Which device is used to measure electric current in a circuit?
questions.explanation: An ammeter measures electric current.

questions.question: Which condition leads to overloading in household circuits?

options.C: Connecting too many appliances to one socket

questions.explanation: Too many appliances draw excessive current, causing overload.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of using MCB instead of a fuse?
questions.explanation: MCBs can be reset after tripping, unlike fuses which must be replaced.

questions.question: Which diagram uses symbols to represent electrical components?
questions.explanation: Circuit diagrams represent electrical components using standard symbols.

questions.question: Which component controls the flow of current in a circuit?
questions.explanation: A switch opens or closes the circuit to control current flow.

questions.question: Which condition allows current to flow in a circuit?
questions.explanation: Current flows only when the circuit is closed.

questions.question: What happens to current if voltage is reduced while resistance stays constant?
questions.explanation: According to Ohm’s law, reducing voltage reduces current.

questions.question: Which part of an electrical plug is connected to the earth wire?
questions.explanation: The top pin in a three-pin plug is the earth connection.

questions.question: Which statement best explains electrical resistance?

options.B: Opposition to the flow of electric current

questions.explanation: Resistance is the opposition offered by a material to the flow of electric current.
